module.title: Module 2: Sources of Laws and Related Presumptions
module.description: Examines the sources and hierarchy of American law, the structure of the federal and Texas court systems, how appellate cases become binding precedent, and the legal presumptions and inferences a surveyor relies on in boundary and title work — made Texas-specific throughout.
learning_objectives: Differentiate among the sources of law (constitutions, statutes, administrative rules, ordinances, common/case law) and explain their hierarchy and supremacy.
Explain the function of legal presumptions and inferences in boundary and property law, and distinguish rebuttable from conclusive presumptions.
Identify the structure of the U.S. and Texas court systems and trace how a boundary case progresses from trial through appeal to become binding case law.
Apply Texas-specific rules — the pure notice recording act (Property Code §§13.001–13.002), the bona fide purchaser doctrine, and Texas's two highest courts — to surveying scenarios.

lessons.title: Sources and Hierarchy of Law
learning_objectives: Name the principal sources of American law and rank them in order of authority.
Explain the doctrine of supremacy and how federal and state jurisdiction divide for boundary disputes.
Identify the Texas codes most relevant to surveying.

content.html:
Why a surveyor must understand sources of law

A boundary is ultimately a legal line, not just a measured one. When two descriptions conflict, or a fence sits across a record line, the surveyor must know which rule of law governs and where that rule comes from. Law in the United States flows from several distinct sources, arranged in a hierarchy. Higher sources control lower ones, and a surveyor who can place a rule in that hierarchy can predict how a court will resolve a conflict. This lesson lays out those sources — constitutions, statutes, administrative rules, local ordinances, and the common/case law built by judges — and then shows how Texas organizes them.

The five principal sources

  • Constitutional law — the highest law. The U.S. Constitution is supreme over all other American law; each state also has its own constitution (Texas's current constitution dates to 1876), supreme within the state but subordinate to the federal Constitution.
  • Statutory law — enactments of a legislative body (Congress or a state legislature). A statute is a written law adopted through the legislative process and organized into topical codes.
  • Administrative law — regulations issued by a state or federal agency acting under authority delegated by statute. In Texas these are collected in the Texas Administrative Code (TAC).
  • Common law — law developed by judges through the decisions of courts and similar tribunals, where no constitution or statute supplies the rule. It governs core property powers: to dispose of property, to have exclusive use, to control resources within it (water, minerals), and to segregate and sell rights.
  • Case law — the collected history of cases decided by judges. Appellate opinions interpret the other sources and, through precedent, themselves become binding law.

rows: Tex. Const. art. V (court structure); navigability principles
Stafford v. King (dignity of calls; footsteps)

content.text: Ordinances are local statutory law — rules adopted by a city council or county commissioners court (e.g., subdivision platting and zoning). They are valid only within that jurisdiction and only to the extent state law authorizes them.

content.html:
Supremacy, jurisdiction, and sovereign immunity

Supremacy means a conflict is resolved in favor of the higher source: federal over state, constitution over statute, statute over administrative rule. Jurisdiction is “the right and power of a court to adjudicate concerning the subject matter in a given case.” For boundaries, jurisdiction tracks title history: a dispute over land that has always been in federal ownership (unpatented public domain) is tried under federal law; once land has passed to private parties by patent, jurisdiction over its boundaries passes to the state courts under state law. Sovereign immunity holds that the government can be sued only when it consents (by statute); common-law remedies such as adverse possession and acquiescence generally cannot be applied against the government. Courts may also take judicial notice of matters of common knowledge (for example, that water flows downhill) without requiring proof.

content.text: Texas differs: Texas land was never federal public domain. Title traces to one of four sovereigns — Spain, Mexico, the Republic of Texas, or the State of Texas — so the original grant is a State (or pre-statehood) patent, never a federal patent. There is no PLSS township-range grid; Texas land is described by metes-and-bounds keyed to an original GLO survey and abstract number.

content.html:
The Texas codes a surveyor lives in

Texas statutes are organized into topical codes. The ones most relevant to boundary practice are:

  • Texas Property Code — estates, conveyances, deeds, easements, restrictive covenants, and the recording acts (Lesson 4).
  • Texas Natural Resources Code — public lands, the General Land Office (GLO), Permanent School Fund lands, and surveys of state land. Once a survey and its field notes are accepted by the GLO, they are legally presumed correct.
  • Texas Civil Practice & Remedies Code, Ch. 16 — adverse possession (§§16.021–16.028) and the limitations period for suits against surveyors (§16.011). A common trap: adverse possession is NOT in the Property Code.
  • Texas Occupations Code, Ch. 1071 (Professional Land Surveying Practices Act) — the licensing enabling act for surveyors.

Administrative rules for surveyors come from TBPELS (the Texas Board of Professional Engineers and Land Surveyors), in 22 TAC, Part 6 — notably Ch. 138 (“Compliance and Professionalism for Surveyors”).

content.text: Texas wrinkle: In 2019 (HB 1523, 86th Leg.) Texas merged its separate engineering and surveying boards into one agency — TBPELS. Many states still keep a stand-alone surveying board. Older TAC chapters (661/663) were repealed and merged into the current Chs. 134/136/138.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Law has a hierarchy: constitution > statute > administrative rule > ordinance, with common/case law filling the gaps and interpreting the rest.
Supremacy resolves conflicts in favor of the higher source; federal over state.
Boundary jurisdiction follows title: federal law for never-patented land, state law once patented.
Sovereign immunity bars common-law boundary remedies (adverse possession, acquiescence) against the government.
Texas surveyors mainly work in the Property Code, Natural Resources Code, Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code Ch. 16, and Occupations Code Ch. 1071, with TBPELS rules in 22 TAC Ch. 138.

flashcards.definition: The right and power of a court to adjudicate concerning the subject matter in a given case.
flashcards.hint_1: Right and POWER of a court.

flashcards.definition: The doctrine that the government may be sued only when it consents; common-law remedies like adverse possession cannot be used against it.
flashcards.hint_1: You can't sue the king without permission.

images_needed.caption: Pyramid/hierarchy of legal authority: Constitution at top, then statutes, administrative rules, ordinances, with common/case law shown interpreting all levels.

lessons.title: The Federal and Texas Court Systems
learning_objectives: Describe the three tiers of courts (trial, appellate, supreme) and what each does.
Contrast the federal court structure with the Texas court structure.
Explain why Texas uniquely has two highest courts and which one decides boundary cases.

content.html:
Three tiers: trial, appellate, supreme

American court systems — federal and state — share a three-tier shape. A case is born in a trial court, may be reviewed by an appellate (intermediate) court, and may finally reach a court of last resort (a supreme court). Understanding this ladder matters to surveyors because only the upper rungs make binding precedent, and a boundary dispute can be appealed only on certain grounds.

rows: Hears a case for the first time; finds the facts and applies the law. Federal trial courts are called district courts.
Reviews a lower court's decision; cannot re-try the facts — only questions of law and procedure. Its opinion binds all courts in its jurisdiction.
Appellant (aggrieved party); Appellee (party appealed against); Respondent
May accept or deny a petition for review; if it accepts and issues an opinion, that opinion binds all courts under its jurisdiction.

content.text: A critical limit on appeals: an appellate court reviews questions of LAW and PROCEDURE, not questions of FACT. In a boundary suit, where a survey line actually lies on the ground is a question of fact decided by the trial court's factfinder — so it is hard to overturn on appeal.

content.html:
The federal court system

The federal judiciary has three levels: the U.S. District Courts (94 districts — the trial courts, each with a bankruptcy court), the U.S. Courts of Appeals (organized into 13 circuits — 12 regional plus the Federal Circuit), and the U.S. Supreme Court at the apex (one court). Specialized federal courts (the Court of International Trade, the Court of Federal Claims) feed into this structure. Federal courts hear boundary disputes over land that has always been federally owned, plus federal questions and diversity cases.

content.alt: Three-tier federal court pyramid with U.S. District Courts at the base, U.S. Courts of Appeals in the middle, and the U.S. Supreme Court at the top.
content.caption: Figure: The federal court system — district courts (trial), courts of appeals (13 circuits), and the U.S. Supreme Court.

content.html:
The Texas court system

Texas mirrors the three-tier shape but with a famous twist. Its trial courts include District Courts (general jurisdiction — boundary and title disputes, trespass-to-try-title, and quiet-title suits start here; Tex. Const. art. V §8), plus County Courts (constitutional and statutory “county courts at law”), Justice (JP) Courts, and Municipal Courts. Intermediate appeals go to the Texas Courts of Appeals. At the top, Texas splits its court of last resort into two co-equal high courts: the Supreme Court of Texas (final authority for civil and juvenile cases — so boundary and title cases end here; 9 members) and the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals (final authority for criminal cases; 9 members).

content.text: Texas differs (and it matters for surveyors): Most states and the federal system have ONE court of last resort for both civil and criminal appeals. Texas splits this into two — a design shared only with Oklahoma. Because boundary and title disputes are CIVIL, the apex authority for boundary precedent is the Supreme Court of Texas; the Court of Criminal Appeals never touches boundary law.

2026 update on the appellate count: Texas historically had 14 Courts of Appeals. As of 2024 there are effectively 15 — the 14 regional districts plus a statewide Fifteenth Court of Appeals (S.B. 1045, 88th Leg. 2023) that hears appeals from the new Texas Business Court and most civil appeals where the State or a state agency/official is a party. Ordinary private boundary appeals still go to the regional court of appeals; older texts say '14.'

links.title: Tex. Const. art. V (Judicial Department)
links.description: Establishes Texas's court structure, including the two highest courts and the district courts' jurisdiction over title/boundary suits.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Courts run in three tiers: trial (finds facts), appellate (reviews law/procedure only), and supreme (discretionary final review).
Federal: U.S. District Courts -> 13 Courts of Appeals circuits -> U.S. Supreme Court.
Texas boundary/title suits begin in District Court (general jurisdiction).
Texas uniquely has TWO highest courts: the Supreme Court of Texas (civil — controls boundary law) and the Court of Criminal Appeals (criminal).
Texas now has effectively 15 courts of appeals (14 regional + the statewide Fifteenth), but private boundary appeals still go to the regional court.

flashcards.definition: The court where a case is heard for the first time; it finds the facts and applies the law. Federal trial courts are called district courts.
A court with jurisdiction to review a lower court's decision; it cannot re-try the facts and reviews only questions of law and procedure. Its opinion binds all courts in its jurisdiction.
The final authority for civil and juvenile cases in Texas (9 members); the apex court for boundary and title precedent.
flashcards.hint_1: Civil court of last resort — not criminal.

flashcards.definition: Texas's separate, co-equal highest court for criminal cases (9 members); never decides boundary law.

images_needed.caption: Side-by-side court hierarchy: federal (District -> Courts of Appeals -> U.S. Supreme Court) next to Texas (JP/Municipal/County/District -> Courts of Appeals -> the two high courts: Supreme Court of Texas and Court of Criminal Appeals).

lessons.title: How a Case Becomes Law: Precedent and Legal Research
learning_objectives: Trace the path by which a trial decision becomes binding appellate precedent.
Distinguish binding precedent from persuasive (advisory) authority and stare decisis.
Use the basic tools of legal research — primary vs. secondary sources, reporters, and case citations.

content.html:
From a single suit to binding precedent

Case law is built one decision at a time. The process is the same whether the dispute is a fence line or a contract: a suit is filed in the proper trial court; the judge rules on the legal issues; that trial ruling binds only the parties to that case and is merely advisory in other cases. If a party believes the trial court erred on a question of law or procedure (but not on the facts), it may appeal. When an appellate court issues an opinion, that opinion binds all courts in its jurisdiction — it becomes precedent. A further appeal may reach the supreme court, whose opinion binds every court beneath it.

Stare decisis and the dignity of precedent

The engine that turns opinions into law is stare decisis — “to stand by things decided.” Courts follow their own prior decisions and those of higher courts in the same jurisdiction so the law stays predictable. Two distinctions matter:

  • Binding (mandatory) authority — a decision a court must follow (a higher court in the same jurisdiction).
  • Persuasive authority — a decision a court may consider but need not follow (a trial-court ruling, an opinion from another jurisdiction, or a treatise).

This is why a single Texas Supreme Court boundary opinion — such as Stafford v. King on the dignity of calls — governs every Texas surveyor's retracement, while a district court's ruling in a neighbor's fence dispute does not.

sections.title: The parts of a legal brief (anatomy of a case)
sections.content: When a surveyor or attorney 'briefs' a case to understand its rule, the standard parts are: (1) case name and citation; (2) facts; (3) procedural posture (what happened at trial and on appeal); (4) issue (the question the appellate court must answer); (5) rule applied; (6) holding (the court's decision specific to this case); (7) reasoning; (8) result; and (9) concurrences and dissents. The holding is the part that becomes precedent.

content.html:
Finding the law: primary vs. secondary sources

Effective research first asks whether the issue is federal or state and what type of primary law applies. A primary source is a statement of the law itself from a governmental entity — a court, legislature, or agency. A secondary source discusses, explains, or interprets the law: treatises, law reviews, legal encyclopedias (Corpus Juris Secundum, American Jurisprudence 2d), and annotations. Because secondary sources are rich with citations to primary law, many researchers begin there. The major online services are Westlaw and LexisNexis. For surveyors, Brown's Boundary Control and Legal Principles is itself a secondary (treatise) source that points to the controlling cases and statutes.

Where opinions live: reporters and citations

Published opinions are collected in reporters. Only appellate-level opinions designated for publication are reported — trial-level decisions are not reported. Federal reporters carry federal opinions; the United States Reports is the official record of the U.S. Supreme Court; the Federal Reporter carries the U.S. Courts of Appeals; the Federal Supplement carries select U.S. District Court opinions. West's regional reporters collect state appellate opinions by region — Texas opinions appear in the South Western Reporter (S.W.). A citation identifies the parties (the style), the volume and reporter, the page, and the year — e.g., Madison v. Gordon, 39 S.W.3d 604 (Tex. 2001) means volume 39 of the South Western Reporter, 3d series, page 604, decided by the Supreme Court of Texas in 2001.

content.text: Reporter geography matters for retracement research. Texas is in the South Western Reporter (S.W./S.W.2d/S.W.3d). New Mexico — and most western states — fall in the Pacific Reporter (P./P.2d/P.3d). Knowing the right regional reporter speeds finding the controlling boundary precedent for the state where the land lies.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A trial ruling binds only its own case; an appellate opinion binds all courts in its jurisdiction and becomes precedent.
Stare decisis makes higher-court decisions binding (mandatory) and other decisions merely persuasive.
Only appellate opinions are reported; trial-court decisions are not published as precedent.
Primary sources state the law (cases, statutes, agency rules); secondary sources (treatises like Brown, encyclopedias, law reviews) explain it and cite to it.
Texas appellate opinions are published in the South Western Reporter; a citation gives parties, volume, reporter, page, court, and year.

flashcards.definition: Latin for 'to stand by things decided'; the doctrine that courts follow prior decisions of higher courts in the same jurisdiction to keep the law consistent.

Binding (mandatory) authority must be followed (a higher court in the same jurisdiction); persuasive authority may be considered but need not be followed (trial rulings, other jurisdictions, treatises).
A primary source is the law itself from a governmental entity (court, legislature, agency); a secondary source discusses or interprets it (treatises, encyclopedias, law reviews).
flashcards.hint_1: The law itself vs. commentary on it.

flashcards.definition: A published set of volumes collecting appellate court opinions; Texas opinions appear in the South Western Reporter (S.W.).

images_needed.caption: Flowchart: suit filed in trial court -> trial ruling (binds only this case) -> appeal on law/procedure -> appellate opinion (binds all courts in jurisdiction = precedent) -> supreme court review.
Anatomy of a case citation labeled: style (parties), volume, reporter abbreviation (S.W.3d), page, and (court year) — using a Texas example.

lessons.title: Recording Acts, Notice, and the Bona Fide Purchaser
learning_objectives: Explain the purpose of recording acts and the difference between actual and constructive notice.
Classify Texas as a pure notice jurisdiction and apply Property Code §§13.001–13.002.
State the elements of the bona fide purchaser doctrine and how possession can defeat it.

content.html:
Why recording acts exist

When the same grantor conveys the same land twice, who wins? At early common law the rule was simply “first in time, first in right” — the earlier deed prevailed. That punished an innocent later buyer who had no way of knowing about a hidden prior deed. Recording acts fix this by giving buyers a public record to search and a set of priority rules that reward those who record (and those who buy without notice). For a surveyor, recording rules explain why the recorded chain of title — not just the deed in the client's hand — controls what was actually conveyed.

Notice: actual and constructive

Recording statutes turn on notice. A purchaser has:

  • Actual notice — real, personal knowledge of a prior claim.
  • Constructive notice — knowledge the law charges a buyer with whether or not they actually have it. A properly recorded instrument is constructive notice to all persons. Open and visible possession of the land can also be constructive notice, putting a buyer on a duty to inquire.

The three statutory regimes that resolve double conveyances are: race (whoever records first wins, even if they knew of the prior deed), notice (a later buyer without notice wins, regardless of recording order), and race-notice (a later buyer wins only if both without notice and first to record).

rows: Later bona fide purchaser without notice
Later BFP without notice AND who records first

content.text: Texas is a PURE NOTICE state. A subsequent purchaser for valuable consideration without notice of a prior unrecorded conveyance takes free of it — and prevails even if the prior grantee records first, or even if the bona fide purchaser never records at all. There is NO recording-priority (race) requirement for the BFP. (In a race-notice state like California, that same buyer would lose without winning the race to the courthouse.)

content.html:
The Texas recording statutes

Two Property Code sections carry the rule:

  • §13.001 (Validity of Unrecorded Instrument) — a conveyance, mortgage, or deed of trust is “void as to a creditor or to a subsequent purchaser for a valuable consideration without notice” unless it is acknowledged and filed for record — but it is still binding on the parties, their heirs, and on a purchaser who does not pay value or who has notice.
  • §13.002 (Effect of Recording) — a properly recorded instrument is “notice to all persons” (constructive notice).
  • §11.001 (Place of Recording) — instruments are recorded in the county where the land lies.

The bona fide purchaser (BFP) doctrine

The buyer protected by the notice statute is the bona fide purchaser. Texas requires three elements: (1) valuable consideration (not nominal — a gift, or a quitclaim-only taker, generally fails); (2) good faith; and (3) without notice, neither actual nor constructive. Constructive notice arises from the records (§13.002) and from possession of the land. The leading Texas case on possession is Madison v. Gordon, 39 S.W.3d 604 (Tex. 2001): possession charges a purchaser with constructive notice only if it is “visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal.” There, a tenant occupying one unit of a fourplex was not exclusive or unequivocal, so it gave no constructive notice and the purchaser prevailed.

content.text: Texas differs on possession and on quitclaims. Texas's four-adjective possession test — 'visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal' — is stricter than the looser 'open and notorious' standard used elsewhere, so ambiguous or shared possession often fails to defeat BFP status. Separately, a grantee under a quitclaim deed is generally NOT a bona fide purchaser, because the quitclaim itself puts them on notice that the grantor's title may be defective.

links.title: Tex. Property Code Ch. 13 (Effects of Recording)
links.description: §13.001 (validity of unrecorded instrument) and §13.002 (effect of recording = notice to all persons) — the heart of Texas's pure notice recording act.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Recording acts replace common-law 'first in time' with a public-record system keyed to notice.
Notice can be actual (real knowledge) or constructive (the record, or open possession).
Texas is a PURE NOTICE state: a BFP without notice wins even if the prior grantee records first or the BFP never records (Prop. Code §§13.001–13.002).
A bona fide purchaser needs valuable consideration, good faith, and no notice; record in the county where the land lies.
Madison v. Gordon: possession is constructive notice only if 'visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal' — a stricter Texas test; quitclaim grantees are generally not BFPs.

flashcards.definition: A statute that establishes priority among competing claims to the same land based on recording and notice, replacing the common-law 'first in time, first in right' rule.
Knowledge the law charges a buyer with regardless of actual awareness; arises from a properly recorded instrument (notice to all persons) and from open possession of the land.
flashcards.hint_1: Notice the law imputes to you.

flashcards.definition: A buyer who acquires land for valuable consideration, in good faith, and without notice (actual or constructive) of a prior claim; protected by the recording act.
Leading Texas case holding that possession is constructive notice only if it is 'visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal.'

images_needed.caption: Double-conveyance timeline: grantor sells to A (unrecorded), then to B (pays value, no notice); decision tree branching by race / notice / race-notice rules, highlighting the Texas notice outcome where B wins.

lessons.title: Presumptions and Inferences in Boundary Law
learning_objectives: Define a legal presumption and distinguish conclusive from rebuttable presumptions.
Distinguish a presumption from an inference and from evidence.
Apply common surveying presumptions and Texas boundary presumptions to retracement problems.

content.html:
What a presumption is — and is not

A presumption is a conclusion the law directs be drawn from certain established facts. Presumptions are not evidence, but they may substitute for evidence where evidence is lacking — which is exactly the surveyor's situation when original monuments are gone and the record is thin. A presumption is conditional on the existence of certain facts; a party who relies on one carries the job of eliminating contrary possibilities. Presumptions may be established by either common law or statute.

Conclusive vs. rebuttable; and inferences

  • Conclusive (irrebuttable) presumptions are absolute — no contrary evidence is allowed to defeat them.
  • Rebuttable presumptions hold until contrary evidence overcomes them. These constitute most of the rules of law that control boundaries — they set the default, but better evidence (a found original monument, the surveyor's footsteps) can override them.

An inference is different. An inference is NOT a presumption; it is a deduction of fact that may be logically and reasonably drawn from another fact or group of facts. A presumption is imposed by law; an inference is reasoned by the factfinder from the evidence.

content.text: Practical mnemonic: a PRESUMPTION is a rule the law hands you ('treat X as true unless disproven'); an INFERENCE is a conclusion you reason out yourself ('these blazed trees and this old fence suggest the original line ran here'). Most boundary presumptions are rebuttable — they yield to superior evidence.

content.html:
Common surveying presumptions

Courts extend a strong set of presumptions to original government surveys and old descriptions. Among the most important for retracement:

  • The original (government) surveyor performed the work competently and according to the laws in effect at the time, and the work was accurate.
  • The survey was made as stated in the field notes, the lines were actually run on the ground, and the corners were established and marked as returned.
  • The field notes and plats are correct, and corners exist at the places the field notes indicate.
  • An ancient survey is presumed correct when so accepted and treated by the interested parties.
  • Land abutting a highway, street, or non-navigable watercourse extends to the center of that feature (the centerline presumption).
  • When a description contains courses and monuments, a survey was actually conducted.

sections.title: A caution on the 'unassailable GLO survey' presumption
sections.content: Lists of surveying presumptions sometimes include that 'an approved U.S. GLO survey is unassailable by any court' and that government corners are presumed correct. These are powerful but they describe the federal PLSS world. In Texas they apply only to the limited federal land in the state. Texas's own original surveys are GLO (General Land Office of Texas) surveys, and the Natural Resources Code gives accepted state surveys and field notes a legal presumption of correctness. The deeper Texas principle, developed in Modules 4-5, is that these presumptions are all subordinate to the intent of the grant and the footsteps of the original surveyor on the ground.

content.html:
Texas boundary presumptions (dignity of calls and more)

Texas boundary law runs on rebuttable presumptions of reliability. The best known is the dignity (priority) of calls: when calls conflict, higher-dignity calls control — (1) natural monuments > (2) artificial monuments > (3) course (bearing) > (4) distance > (5) quantity/area. The foundational Texas case is Stafford v. King, 30 Tex. 257 (1867) (full treatment in Modules 4-5). Other Texas presumptions a surveyor uses: the centerline / center-of-road presumption (land bounded “by” or “along” a road or non-navigable stream carries title to the center, subject to the public easement, unless clearly excluded) and the strip-and-gore doctrine (a grantor conveying land abutting a narrow strip is presumed not to retain that strip if it has no practical use apart from the conveyed tract).

content.text: Texas key point: The dignity-of-calls hierarchy is only a RULE OF CONSTRUCTION — a set of rebuttable reliability presumptions. It always yields to (i) the intent of the parties (paramount) and (ii) the footsteps of the original surveyor where they can be found on the ground. A found, identified original monument controls over a conflicting field-note call.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A presumption is a conclusion the law directs from established facts; it is not evidence but can substitute for it when evidence is missing.
Conclusive presumptions are absolute; rebuttable presumptions (most boundary rules) yield to superior evidence.
An inference is a fact-deduction reasoned by the factfinder — not a presumption imposed by law.
Surveys, field notes, corners, and ancient surveys carry strong presumptions of correctness; land abutting a road/non-navigable stream is presumed to run to the center.
The Texas dignity of calls (natural > artificial > course > distance > quantity) is a rebuttable rule of construction subordinate to grant intent and the original surveyor's footsteps.

flashcards.definition: A conclusion the law expressly directs to be drawn from certain established facts; not evidence, but it may substitute for evidence where evidence is lacking.
flashcards.hint_1: A rule of law, not proof.

flashcards.definition: A conclusive presumption is irrebuttable and absolute; a rebuttable presumption holds until overcome by contrary evidence and accounts for most boundary rules.
A deduction of fact that may be logically and reasonably drawn from another fact or group of facts; it is NOT a presumption.
flashcards.hint_1: You reason it; the law doesn't impose it.

flashcards.definition: The Texas priority of conflicting calls: natural monuments > artificial monuments > course (bearing) > distance > quantity/area; a rebuttable rule of construction.
flashcards.hint_1: Natural over artificial over course over distance.

images_needed.caption: Two-column comparison graphic: PRESUMPTION (imposed by law; rebuttable vs. conclusive) vs. INFERENCE (reasoned from facts), with a boundary example under each.
Dignity-of-calls ladder showing natural monuments at top descending to quantity/area at bottom, with a note that intent and footsteps override the ladder.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which source of law sits at the top of the American legal hierarchy and is supreme over all others?
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The U.S. Constitution
quiz_questions.explanation: The U.S. Constitution is the highest law of the land; all statutes, rules, and ordinances are subordinate to it. State statutes, agency regulations, and local ordinances each rank below it.

quiz_questions.question_text: Law developed by judges through the decisions of courts, where no constitution or statute supplies the rule, is called:
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Common law
quiz_questions.explanation: Common law is judge-made law from court decisions. Statutory law comes from legislatures, administrative law from agency regulations, and constitutional law from constitutions.

quiz_questions.question_text: A boundary dispute concerns land that has always been in federal ownership and was never patented. Whose law governs the dispute?
options: State law, because boundaries are always a state matter
Federal law, because the land was never severed from federal ownership
Local ordinance, because the county records the survey
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Federal law, because the land was never severed from federal ownership
quiz_questions.explanation: Jurisdiction tracks title history. Unpatented (never-private) land is tried under federal law; once land passes to private parties by patent, boundary jurisdiction passes to the state courts under state law.

quiz_questions.question_text: True or False: Under sovereign immunity, common-law remedies such as adverse possession and acquiescence can generally be applied against the government to gain title to its land.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: Sovereign immunity means the government can be sued only when it consents. Common-law remedies like adverse possession and acquiescence generally cannot be used to take title from the government.

quiz_questions.question_text: In Texas, which code contains the adverse-possession statutes — a frequent point of confusion?
options: The Texas Civil Practice & Remedies Code, Chapter 16
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The Texas Civil Practice & Remedies Code, Chapter 16
quiz_questions.explanation: Adverse possession is in Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code Ch. 16 (§§16.021–16.028) — NOT the Property Code, a common trap. The Property Code covers conveyances and recording; the Natural Resources Code covers public lands; Occupations Code Ch. 1071 covers surveyor licensing.

quiz_questions.question_text: Texas's surveyor standards of practice are administrative rules adopted by TBPELS. Where are they found?
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 22 Texas Administrative Code, Chapter 138
quiz_questions.explanation: TBPELS rules, including 'Compliance and Professionalism for Surveyors,' are in 22 TAC Ch. 138. The Constitution is higher-tier law; the South Western Reporter holds case opinions; the U.S. Code is federal statutes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A surveyor's client lost at trial and wants to appeal because the judge believed the opposing surveyor's testimony about where the line lies on the ground. What is the main obstacle to that appeal?
options: Appellate courts can only review questions of law and procedure, not re-decide questions of fact
Boundary cases cannot be appealed at all
Only the losing surveyor, not the client, may appeal
The appeal must go first to the Court of Criminal Appeals
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Appellate courts can only review questions of law and procedure, not re-decide questions of fact
quiz_questions.explanation: Where a line lies on the ground is a question of FACT for the trial factfinder. Appellate courts review only questions of law and procedure, so factual findings are hard to overturn. Boundary cases can be appealed (on law/procedure); the aggrieved party (the client) appeals; and civil appeals never go to the Court of Criminal Appeals.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why is the Supreme Court of Texas — not the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals — the apex authority for boundary precedent?
options: Because boundary disputes are civil cases, and the Supreme Court of Texas is the final authority for civil cases
Because the Court of Criminal Appeals has fewer judges
Because boundary cases skip the intermediate courts
Because the Court of Criminal Appeals only hears federal questions
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Because boundary disputes are civil cases, and the Supreme Court of Texas is the final authority for civil cases
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas splits its court of last resort: the Supreme Court of Texas decides civil (including boundary/title) cases, and the Court of Criminal Appeals decides criminal cases. Since boundary disputes are civil, they end at the Supreme Court of Texas. Both high courts have 9 members.

quiz_questions.question_text: True or False: Texas and Oklahoma are unusual because each has two separate highest courts — one for civil cases and one for criminal cases.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: True
quiz_questions.explanation: Most states and the federal system have a single court of last resort. Only Texas and Oklahoma split it into two co-equal high courts: a civil supreme court and a criminal court of appeals.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the federal court system, which courts are the trial courts where a boundary case over federal land would first be heard?
quiz_questions.correct_answer: U.S. District Courts
quiz_questions.explanation: U.S. District Courts (94 districts) are the federal trial courts. The U.S. Courts of Appeals (13 circuits) are intermediate appellate courts, and the U.S. Supreme Court is the apex. There is no federal 'Court of Criminal Appeals.'

quiz_questions.question_text: In which Texas trial court do title and boundary disputes (trespass-to-try-title, quiet title) generally begin?
quiz_questions.correct_answer: District Court (general jurisdiction)
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas District Courts have general jurisdiction, including title and boundary suits (Tex. Const. art. V §8). JP and municipal courts are limited-jurisdiction courts; the Fifteenth Court of Appeals is an appellate court, not a trial court.

quiz_questions.question_text: A trial court in a neighboring county ruled on a fence-line dispute much like your client's. How does that trial ruling affect your client's separate case?
options: It is binding precedent that the court must follow
It binds only the parties to that case and is merely advisory (persuasive) in other cases
It automatically reverses any conflicting appellate opinion
quiz_questions.correct_answer: It binds only the parties to that case and is merely advisory (persuasive) in other cases
quiz_questions.explanation: A trial ruling binds only its own parties and is only advisory elsewhere. Binding precedent comes from appellate opinions within the jurisdiction. Trial rulings cannot reverse appellate opinions, but they may still be considered as persuasive authority.

quiz_questions.question_text: The doctrine that courts stand by prior decisions of higher courts in the same jurisdiction, keeping the law predictable, is called:
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Stare decisis
quiz_questions.explanation: Stare decisis ('to stand by things decided') is the precedent-following doctrine. Res judicata bars relitigating a decided claim between the same parties; sovereign immunity protects the government from suit; judicial notice accepts common-knowledge facts without proof.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a SECONDARY legal source rather than a primary one?
options: Brown's Boundary Control and Legal Principles (a treatise)
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Brown's Boundary Control and Legal Principles (a treatise)
quiz_questions.explanation: A treatise discusses and interprets the law, so it is a secondary source. Court opinions, statutes (the Property Code), and agency rules are primary sources — statements of the law itself from governmental entities.

quiz_questions.question_text: True or False: Trial-level court decisions are routinely published in the regional reporters as binding precedent.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: Only appellate-level opinions designated for publication appear in reporters. Trial-level decisions are generally not reported and do not serve as binding precedent.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the citation Madison v. Gordon, 39 S.W.3d 604 (Tex. 2001), what does '604' indicate?
options: The year the case was decided
The page on which the opinion begins
The number of the court district
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The page on which the opinion begins
quiz_questions.explanation: In a citation, the number after the reporter abbreviation is the starting page. '39' is the volume, 'S.W.3d' is the reporter (South Western Reporter, 3d series), '604' is the page, and '(Tex. 2001)' is the court and year.

quiz_questions.question_text: Texas appellate opinions are collected primarily in which West regional reporter?
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The South Western Reporter
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas (along with Arkansas, Kentucky, Missouri, and Tennessee) appears in the South Western Reporter (S.W.). New Mexico and most western states are in the Pacific Reporter; the Atlantic and North Eastern reporters cover other regions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the defining feature of a PURE NOTICE recording jurisdiction like Texas?
options: The first party to record always wins, even with notice
A later purchaser for value without notice wins, even if the prior grantee records first or the buyer never records
A later purchaser wins only if they record before everyone else
Recording has no legal effect at all
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A later purchaser for value without notice wins, even if the prior grantee records first or the buyer never records
quiz_questions.explanation: In a pure notice state, the bona fide purchaser without notice prevails regardless of recording order — there is no race requirement. 'First to record wins' describes a race state; 'must record first' describes race-notice; and recording certainly has legal effect (it gives constructive notice).

quiz_questions.question_text: A grantor deeds Blackacre to A, who does not record. The grantor then deeds the same land to B, who pays full value, has no knowledge of A's deed, and finds nothing in the records. A records the next week, before B records. Under Texas law, who owns Blackacre?
options: B, because B is a bona fide purchaser without notice and Texas is a pure notice state
A, because A's deed was first in time
Neither — the land reverts to the grantor
quiz_questions.correct_answer: B, because B is a bona fide purchaser without notice and Texas is a pure notice state
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas is a pure notice state. B paid value without notice of A's unrecorded deed, so B prevails — and the fact that A records first is irrelevant because there is no race requirement. Under §13.001, A's unrecorded deed is void as to a subsequent purchaser for value without notice.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under Tex. Property Code §13.002, what is the effect of properly recording an instrument?
options: It makes the instrument valid even if forged
It is notice to all persons (constructive notice)
It exempts the buyer from paying value
quiz_questions.correct_answer: It is notice to all persons (constructive notice)
quiz_questions.explanation: §13.002 provides that a properly recorded instrument is notice to all persons — i.e., constructive notice. Recording does not cure forgery, does not by itself transfer title, and does not change the value requirement.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which set of elements must a buyer satisfy to be a bona fide purchaser in Texas?
options: Valuable consideration, good faith, and without notice (actual or constructive)
Any consideration, first to record, and a quitclaim deed
Knowledge of the prior claim, value, and good faith
Possession of the land for ten years
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Valuable consideration, good faith, and without notice (actual or constructive)
quiz_questions.explanation: A BFP needs (1) valuable (not nominal) consideration, (2) good faith, and (3) no notice — actual or constructive. A buyer with knowledge of the prior claim cannot be a BFP, and a quitclaim-only taker generally fails the doctrine.

quiz_questions.question_text: In Madison v. Gordon (Tex. 2001), possession charges a purchaser with constructive notice only if the possession is described by which four adjectives?
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal
quiz_questions.explanation: Madison v. Gordon, 39 S.W.3d 604 (Tex. 2001), holds that possession is constructive notice only if it is 'visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal.' This four-adjective test is stricter than the looser 'open and notorious' standard used in many other states.

quiz_questions.question_text: True or False: In Texas, a grantee who receives land by quitclaim deed is generally treated as a bona fide purchaser entitled to full BFP protection.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: A quitclaim grantee in Texas is generally NOT a bona fide purchaser, because the quitclaim itself puts the grantee on notice that the grantor's title may be defective. This is stricter than many states, which treat a quitclaim grantee like any other purchaser.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement best describes a legal presumption?
options: It is a form of direct evidence the surveyor must collect
It is a conclusion the law directs from established facts; it is not evidence but may substitute for evidence when evidence is lacking
It is a personal opinion of the surveyor with no legal weight
It is the same thing as an inference
quiz_questions.correct_answer: It is a conclusion the law directs from established facts; it is not evidence but may substitute for evidence when evidence is lacking
quiz_questions.explanation: A presumption is a legal conclusion drawn from established facts. It is not itself evidence, but it can stand in for evidence when none is available. It is distinct from an inference, which is reasoned by the factfinder rather than imposed by law.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does an inference differ from a presumption?
options: An inference is imposed by law; a presumption is reasoned by the factfinder
An inference is a fact-deduction reasoned from other facts; a presumption is a conclusion the law directs
An inference is always conclusive; a presumption is always rebuttable
quiz_questions.correct_answer: An inference is a fact-deduction reasoned from other facts; a presumption is a conclusion the law directs
quiz_questions.explanation: An inference is a logical deduction of fact the factfinder draws from the evidence; a presumption is a conclusion the law directs from established facts. They are not the same, and presumptions — not inferences — may be conclusive or rebuttable.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which kind of presumption accounts for most of the rules of law that control boundaries?
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Rebuttable presumptions
quiz_questions.explanation: Most boundary rules are rebuttable presumptions — they set the default but yield to superior evidence such as a found original monument or the surveyor's footsteps. Conclusive presumptions are absolute and far less common in boundary work.

quiz_questions.question_text: A deed describes land as bounded 'along an old county road' (a non-navigable feature) with no express reservation of the roadbed. Which presumption typically applies in Texas?
options: The grantor is presumed to have retained the entire roadbed
Title is presumed to extend to the center of the road, subject to the public easement
Title is presumed to stop at the near edge of the road
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Title is presumed to extend to the center of the road, subject to the public easement
quiz_questions.explanation: The centerline presumption: land bounded by or along a road, street, or non-navigable watercourse is presumed to carry title to the center, subject to the public easement, unless clearly excluded. This is a rebuttable presumption.

quiz_questions.question_text: True or False: In Texas, the dignity-of-calls hierarchy (natural > artificial > course > distance > quantity) is an absolute rule that always controls, regardless of the grant's intent or the original surveyor's footsteps.
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: The dignity of calls is only a rebuttable rule of construction. It always yields to (i) the intent of the parties (paramount) and (ii) the footsteps of the original surveyor where they can be found on the ground. A found original monument controls over a conflicting field-note call.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under the common surveying presumptions, which of the following is presumed when a description contains courses and monuments?
options: That no survey was ever performed
That a survey was actually conducted
That the description is automatically void
That the land must be re-platted before sale
quiz_questions.correct_answer: That a survey was actually conducted
quiz_questions.explanation: A standard surveying presumption is that when a description contains courses and monuments, a survey was actually conducted. Related presumptions hold that the lines were run on the ground and the corners were established and marked as returned.

homework.title: Homework 1 — Trace a Texas Boundary Dispute Through the Court System and the Sources of Law
homework.prompt:
**Written assignment (non-computational). Answer in complete sentences; cite the relevant Texas code sections and the case from the lesson where appropriate.**

A landowner in a rural Texas county hires you to retrace a boundary. You discover that the neighbor's recently recorded deed overlaps your client's older (but previously **unrecorded**) deed by about 15 feet, and the neighbor's fence sits on the disputed strip. The neighbor claims to have bought in good faith for full value with no knowledge of your client's deed.

1. **Sources of law (Lesson 1).** List, in order of authority, the sources of law that could bear on this dispute (constitution, statutes, administrative rules, ordinances, case law). For each, give one concrete example relevant to *this* boundary problem (e.g., which Texas code governs recording, which governs surveyor conduct).
2. **The court path (Lessons 2-3).** In which Texas trial court would a suit to resolve this dispute begin, and why? Trace the case up through the appellate level to the correct court of last resort. Explain *why* the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals is **not** the final authority here. Identify one issue in the case that is a **question of fact** (hard to appeal) and one that is a **question of law** (appealable).
3. **Recording and the BFP (Lesson 4).** Texas is a pure **notice** jurisdiction. Apply **Tex. Property Code §§13.001 and §13.002** to decide, on these facts, whether the neighbor likely qualifies as a **bona fide purchaser** and what the consequence is for your client's unrecorded deed. Then explain how the rule of **Madison v. Gordon** (possession as constructive notice) could change the outcome — specifically, what about the **fence/possession** might have charged the neighbor with constructive notice, defeating BFP status.
4. **Reflection.** In two or three sentences, explain how a different recording regime (a **race-notice** state) might produce a different result on the same facts.

*Length: roughly 1.5-2 pages.*

homework.title: Homework 2 — Presumptions, Inferences, and the Dignity of Calls in Retracement
homework.prompt:
**Written assignment (non-computational). Answer in complete sentences.**

1. **Presumption vs. inference.** In your own words, define a legal **presumption** and a legal **inference**, and explain the key difference (which is imposed by law, and which is reasoned from facts). Give one boundary-surveying example of each. Then explain why a presumption is said to be 'not evidence' yet can 'substitute for evidence.'
2. **Conclusive vs. rebuttable.** Explain the difference between a conclusive and a rebuttable presumption, and state which type accounts for **most** of the rules that control boundaries. Why does that matter to a surveyor who finds a monument that conflicts with the record?
3. **Apply the common surveying presumptions.** You are retracing an old survey. The original field notes describe courses and monuments, but several monuments are missing. List **four** of the 'common surveying presumptions' from the lesson that you could rely on, and explain how each helps you reconstruct the original line. Address how the presumption that an **ancient survey is correct when accepted and treated as such by the parties** could support your work.
4. **Dignity of calls and the Texas overrides.** A single Texas deed contains conflicting calls: a call to a **natural monument** (a creek), a **course** (a bearing), and a **distance**. State the order in which these calls control under the dignity-of-calls hierarchy. Then explain the **two things** that override the entire hierarchy in Texas (hint: one concerns the parties' intent; the other concerns the original surveyor). Conclude by explaining, in two or three sentences, why a *found and identified* original monument on the ground will control over a conflicting field-note call.

*Length: roughly 1.5-2 pages.*
